title: Pool capacity check and worker acquisition are not atomic: two concurrent acquireSlot calls can both pass tryAcquireIdle/acquire and both get null, or both pass and race to consume the last slot
description: After the admission gate await (line 343), control returns to the event loop microtask queue. If two concurrent acquireSlot calls both await the admission gate and then both reach tryAcquireIdle at line 355, the first drains the last idle worker and returns it, but the second also calls pool.acquire() at line 363 which awaits spawnAndReserve. Because spawning is guarded by totalAllocatedOrSpawning >= maxSize, a second concurrent spawn is usually blocked. However in the path where tryAcquireIdle returns null for both callers simultaneously and they both call pool.acquire(), each gets a separate spawn promise. The spawning counter is incremented before the await so over-spawn protection works, but the window between tryAcquireIdle returning null and pool.acquire() being called means both callers enter the async spawn path concurrently. This is the intended design, but the pattern creates subtle issues: if both spawns complete near-simultaneously, only one gets a reserved slot (reserve() checks destroyed/terminated/cancelling), but the second spawned worker goes idle without being immediately used, wasting the spawn latency.

title: The async drainQueue IIFE is launched with void and continues running after scheduler.shutdown() resolves, creating a task that can access destroyed scheduler state
description: The async IIFE at line 1363 is started with `void (async () => { ... })()`. When shutdown() is called, it sets `this.destroyed = true` and calls `await this.pool.shutdown()`. However, an in-flight drainQueue async IIFE that has already passed the `this.queue.isEmpty || this.drainingQueue` check but has not yet awaited `this.pool.acquire()` will continue executing after `pool.shutdown()` resolves. The IIFE checks `this.destroyed` after each pool.acquire() call only inside the catch branch (line 1390), but the main while loop at line 1365 only checks `!this.queue.isEmpty`. If `pool.acquire()` returns null due to shutdown and the queue still has entries (which were not drained by the shutdown loop because the IIFE holds drainingQueue=true), the IIFE will exit cleanly. However during the window between shutdown() setting destroyed=true and pool.shutdown() completing, the drainQueue IIFE can call `this.pool.acquire()` on an already-destroying pool, potentially getting a worker from an intermediate state. Additionally `this.drainingQueue` is set to true by the IIFE and reset in `finally`, but shutdown() does not wait for the IIFE to finish — so `this.drainingQueue` may be true when sessions.clear() runs, and then the IIFE's finally block sets it false on a cleared scheduler.

title: tryPreempt awaits ack from victim but does not re-check if the incoming session was already cancelled during the await window
description: tryPreempt awaits a pause/cancel ack from the victim worker (line 891). This await can take up to 2 seconds (timeout). During this window, the params.sessionId session that triggered preemption could be cancelled via cancelSession(). The caller (acquireSlot) does check cancelledDuringAcquisition after the await at lines 380-384, but only if tryPreempt returns non-null. If tryPreempt has already called assignWorker (line 911) before returning, the session is already bound to a worker but cancelSession was already called, meaning the cancel was recorded in cancelledDuringAcquisition. The ghost guard at line 381 will fire, call abortAcquisition, but by that point the session record has been created with state='running' by assignWorker. abortAcquisition calls this.releaseAdmission (fine) and this.dedupe.complete (fine if sourceKey set), then throws. But the session record created by assignWorker at lines 1125-1136 is never cleaned up: worker remains bound, no decode_cancel is sent, and the worker slot leaks until the pool shuts down.

title: recycle() fires cleanupAndRemove() as a void-unawaited promise; callers that call recycle() then immediately check pool.size get stale results
description: recycle() at line 280 calls `void this.cleanupAndRemove(worker)`. cleanupAndRemove synchronously removes the worker from all maps (idle, active, parked, workers) on lines 626-631, so pool.size reflects the removal immediately. However the actual worker.handle.shutdown() call at line 641 is async and not awaited. If shutdown() throws or takes a long time, there is no error propagation and no backpressure. More concretely: the budget release on line 636 (`this.coreBudget.release(cost)`) happens synchronously in cleanupAndRemove, which can unblock a waiting CoreBudget.acquire() before the worker thread has actually terminated. A new worker can be spawned before the old (recycled) worker has finished shutting down, momentarily exceeding the intended concurrency bound if the new worker is also a budget-cost worker.

category: task leak / resource leak on error path
title: spawn() acquires a CoreBudget token before the spawning counter is incremented; a concurrent shutdown() between acquire() and spawning++ can leave the token permanently consumed
description: In spawn() at line 414, the budget is acquired at line 421 (`await this.coreBudget.acquire(this.workerCost)`), and acquiredBudget is set true. The spawning counter is incremented at line 426 only after the budget await resolves. If shutdown() is called between the budget.acquire() await resolving and the spawning++ line, `this.destroyed` becomes true. The subsequent check at line 466 (`if (this.destroyed || generation !== this.generation)`) causes the newly spawned worker to be shut down, and the catch block at line 443 releases the budget. So for the normal error/destroy path, the budget is released correctly. However the sequence in spawnInner at lines 453-478 shows: if `this.destroyed` is true at line 466, it shuts down the handle but still returns the worker object (without adding it to this.workers). The caller (spawnAndReserve) then checks `this.workers.has(worker.id)` at line 405 — this is false, so it calls destroyWorker and returns null. The budget release in the catch block of spawn() fires only if an exception is thrown. But spawnInner does NOT throw when destroyed; it returns normally. So the finally block runs (spawning--, spawnPromises.delete), and the budget release in the try block at lines 434-439 fires only if `this.workers.has(worker.id)` is false. Tracing: line 434 checks workers.has — false (destroyed path didn't add it) → line 438 releases budget. This looks correct on careful reading. Filing as low-confidence but notable for the subtle control flow.

title: Pause-path in tryPreempt does pool.park then pool.unpark immediately in sequence, bypassing park's intended lifecycle; worker is in RESERVED state while the paused session record still references it via pausedOnWorker
description: In the pause branch (line 939), the code calls `this.pool.park(backgroundWorker)` at line 952 which moves the worker to the parked set and clears activeSessionId to null. Immediately after (line 953), `this.pool.unpark(backgroundWorker)` removes it from the parked set without adding it anywhere else. Then line 954 sets `backgroundWorker.activeSessionId = RESERVED_SESSION_ID`. This sequence puts the worker in a transient state: not in idle, not in active, not in parked, but present in the workers map. This is intentional — the worker is being repurposed. However, workerPausedSession.set() at line 951 maps worker.id → victimSessionId, and victimRecord.pausedOnWorker is set to backgroundWorker. If any message arrives from the worker in this RESERVED intermediate state (between park() and assignWorker()), handleWorkerMessage will be called with sessionId = RESERVED_SESSION_ID (line 1167), which is filtered and dropped. This is safe. But the park/unpark ping-pong is confusing and the parked set invariant is violated transiently; the pool's assertInvariants() in DEV mode only runs at method boundaries, not mid-sequence, so it won't catch the transient state.

title: If acquireSlot throws (via abortAcquisition) after dedupe.register() has been called but before setupSignalAbort(), the sourceKey is removed from the dedupe registry but no cancelledDuringAcquisition tombstone is added
description: abortAcquisition() at line 266 calls dedupe.complete() to remove the key→primary mapping and then throws. The sessionId is never added to cancelledDuringAcquisition (that only happens in cancelSession when there is no record). If the calling code had already registered this sessionId as a primary via dedupe.register() (line 336), and abortAcquisition fires (e.g., signal.aborted check at line 346 or cancelled-during-acquisition at line 347), the dedupe entry is cleaned up correctly. However any subscribers that were notified in abortAcquisition's loop (lines 271-283) have their cleanupSession called synchronously, but the primary's record is only in dedupe (not yet in sessions map), so cleanupSession's sessions.delete at line 1303 is a no-op. The admission gate release is called (line 267). The primary's abort listener is never registered (setupSignalAbort happens after this path). This appears mostly safe, but a future code change that adds any session-map state between dedupe.register() and the abort checks could create a leak.

title: completeSession calls cleanupSession then drainQueue, but drainQueue's async IIFE can still be running from a previous call; if both the old IIFE and completeSession/handleWorkerMessage both call drainQueue, the drainingQueue guard prevents double-entry but the new drainQueue call silently no-ops
description: When handleWorkerMessage receives a terminal message (line 1226), it calls cleanupSession then either resumePausedSession or pool.release + drainQueue. If an async drainQueue IIFE from a previous worker completion is already in-flight (drainingQueue=true), the new drainQueue call at line 1233 returns immediately at the guard check (line 1322). This means the queued session that should be dispatched next waits until the existing IIFE loop processes it. This is the intended behavior. However if the existing IIFE calls pool.acquire() and gets null (because all workers are now busy with the new assignment), it breaks out of its while loop and sets drainingQueue=false in finally. At this point, the session that should have been dispatched by the second drainQueue call is still sitting in the queue but no one wakes it up — it will only be dispatched when the NEXT worker completes. This is a latency issue rather than a correctness bug: sessions can be unnecessarily delayed by one full worker lifetime when the async drain IIFE and a synchronous drain trigger interleave.

title: setupSignalAbort has a race: if signal fires between the aborted check and addEventListener, the abort listener is registered but never fires for an already-aborted signal
description: setupSignalAbort checks `signal.aborted` at line 1259 synchronously. If the signal is not aborted, it registers an addEventListener at line 1264. In a browser environment, AbortSignal.abort() is synchronous — once `signal.abort()` is called, `signal.aborted` becomes true immediately and the 'abort' event fires synchronously on all registered listeners. However there is a window between the check at line 1259 and the addEventListener at line 1264. If `signal.abort()` is called between these two lines (in a microtask or in a nested call from the same tick), the addEventListener is registered AFTER the event fired and `{ once: true }` means it will only fire once — on the NEXT abort, which never comes. The session becomes a zombie: still in the sessions map, never cancelled. In JavaScript's single-threaded model this race cannot happen within a single synchronous block, but if the setupSignalAbort call is inside a microtask continuation (which it is — called after awaits), and the AbortController.abort() is called from another microtask scheduled before this one, the signal could already be aborted by the time this code runs. The existing `signal.aborted` check prevents the most common case, but a microtask-race between abort() and addEventListener() is theoretically possible.

title: spawnStaggered is fire-and-forget via void; if the pool is destroyed mid-stagger, the loop breaks but the Promise itself is not tracked in spawnPromises, so shutdown's Promise.allSettled does not wait for it
description: prewarm() calls `void this.spawnStaggered(toSpawn)`. The spawnStaggered async function is not added to spawnPromises. shutdown() at line 355 awaits `Promise.allSettled([...this.spawnPromises])` to drain in-flight spawns, but spawnStaggered is not in that set. Each individual spawn inside the loop does add its inner promise to spawnPromises (via this.spawn() which calls spawnInner() which creates and adds to spawnPromises), so individual spawn promises ARE tracked. However, the stagger timer itself and the loop continuation (the `await new Promise<void>` at line 312) are not tracked. If shutdown() is called while a stagger timer is pending, it clears the timer (line 349-352) and the spawnStaggered loop breaks at the next `if (this.destroyed) break`. But if the loop is currently awaiting the stagger timer future (which was cleared), that future will never resolve, leaving spawnStaggered hanging... Actually wait: clearTimeout fires the promise's 'never resolves' path. The timer callback sets staggerTimer=null and calls r(), but clearTimeout prevents the callback — so the inner new Promise never resolves. This means spawnStaggered hangs forever on a cleared timer. The stagger timer is correctly cleared in shutdownInner (line 349), but the async stagger loop's pending Promise.resolve is abandoned, not hung — since r() is never called when the timer is cleared. The stagger Promise leaks as an unresolved promise.

title: CoreBudget.acquire() checks tokens and returns synchronously if sufficient, but between the check and the return, another synchronous caller can also see the same tokens value and both take the same tokens
description: acquire() at line 39 checks `if (this.tokens >= cost)` and immediately decrements and returns. In JavaScript's single-threaded event loop, two synchronous calls cannot interleave — but if acquire() is called from a microtask (e.g., inside a .then() continuation) while another microtask resolves and also calls acquire(), the event loop processes them sequentially, not concurrently. So this is not a real race for the JS single-threaded case. However, the pattern of checking then decrementing is only safe because JS is single-threaded. The comments in the file make no mention of this invariant. If this code were ever ported to a SharedArrayBuffer-based context (Atomics), this pattern would be a genuine TOCTOU. This is documented as a low-severity awareness item rather than an actionable bug in the current runtime.

title: acquireSlot has four separate post-await cancelled-guard checks that could be unified into a single cancellation token checked by helper
description: acquireSlot() checks cancelledDuringAcquisition and signal.aborted at three separate points (lines 346-349, 364-369, 380-385), each requiring the same three-statement pattern. The pattern could be extracted into a single `throwIfCancelled()` helper that checks destroyed, signal.aborted, and cancelledDuringAcquisition atomically. This would reduce future bugs when new await points are added to the acquisition path, since a developer would only need to insert `throwIfCancelled()` rather than repeating the three-condition check. Currently missing the check between tryPreempt's internal pool.acquire() calls (lines 909, 929, 991, 1003) — those paths call assignWorker directly without rechecking cancellation.

category: no backpressure mechanism on producer-consumer path
title: waitForDrain backpressure state is per-session but queueDepth is never reset when the session transitions from queued to running; buffered chunks accumulate unboundedly before the worker is assigned
description: When a session is queued (state='queued'), send() buffers chunks into pending.bufferedChunks (line 442). There is no backpressure for queued sessions — waitForDrain only guards the running-session path (line 673 returns immediately if record is undefined or backpressure is undefined). Once the session transitions to running via assignWorker, bufferedChunks are flushed synchronously (lines 1343-1345 or 1375-1377). If the producer is calling send() and waitForDrain() rapidly before a worker is assigned, the backpressure check always returns immediately (no backpressure record exists for queued sessions) and bufferedChunks can grow without bound. This is an existing design limitation: implementing backpressure for queued sessions would require either a shared backpressure record or a pre-queue send() gate.
